coredata.dc:title: Metallic deep eutectic solvents-assisted synthesis of Cu, Cl-doped carbon dots as oxidase-like and peroxidase-like nanozyme for colorimetric assay of hydroquinone and H2O2

Song, Jianqiang
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: As an emerging carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn significant attention in the catalytic field owing to their low toxicity, high stability and superior biocompatibility. Herein, for the first time, Cu and Cl co-doped CDs (Cu, Cl-CDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal method utilizing citric acid as carbon source and metallic deep eutectic solvents (MDESs) as green solvent and passivation agent. It was proved that the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs exhibited oxidase-like (OD-like) and peroxidase-like (POD-like) catalytic activities. The catalytic performance, mechanism and steady-state kinetics of Cu, Cl-CDs were systematically investigated. Accordingly, bifunctional colorimetric platforms based on the OD-like and POD-like activities of Cu, Cl-CDs using 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as substrate were established for determining hydroquinone (HQ) and H2O2, respectively, both of which showed high accuracy and sensitivity. HQ, as a reducing agent, could be detected by this method with a linear range of 0.1–120 µM and limit of detection (LOD) of 0.08 µM. And the degree of discoloration varied linearly with H2O2 in the range of 1–600 µM with LOD of 0.35 µM. Thus, Cu, Cl-CDs have broad prospects in catalysis and environmental chemistry, and provide rational guidance for green synthesis of carbon-based nanozymes with multi-enzyme catalytic performance.

head.ce:title: Metallic deep eutectic solvents-assisted synthesis of Cu, Cl-doped carbon dots as oxidase-like and peroxidase-like nanozyme for colorimetric assay of hydroquinone and H2O2

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an emerging carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn significant attention in the catalytic field owing to their low toxicity, high stability and superior biocompatibility. Herein, for the first time, Cu and Cl co-doped CDs (Cu, Cl-CDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal method utilizing citric acid as carbon source and metallic deep eutectic solvents (MDESs) as green solvent and passivation agent. It was proved that the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs exhibited oxidase-like (OD-like) and peroxidase-like (POD-like) catalytic activities. The catalytic performance, mechanism and steady-state kinetics of Cu, Cl-CDs were systematically investigated. Accordingly, bifunctional colorimetric platforms based on the OD-like and POD-like activities of Cu, Cl-CDs using 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as substrate were established for determining hydroquinone (HQ) and H2O2, respectively, both of which showed high accuracy and sensitivity. HQ, as a reducing agent, could be detected by this method with a linear range of 0.1–120 µM and limit of detection (LOD) of 0.08 µM. And the degree of discoloration varied linearly with H2O2 in the range of 1–600 µM with LOD of 0.35 µM. Thus, Cu, Cl-CDs have broad prospects in catalysis and environmental chemistry, and provide rational guidance for green synthesis of carbon-based nanozymes with multi-enzyme catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes (nanozymes) have received great interest, owing to their unique preponderance of controllable synthesis, low cost, high stability and easy large-scale preparation, in comparison with natural enzymes [1]. Inspired by these advantages, various nanozymes with intrinsic enzyme mimetic activities including nanoparticles [2,3], nanosheets [4,5], nanoclusters [6,7], carbon nanomaterials [8,9] and nanocomposites [10,11] have been prepared, and are widely used as ideal substitutes for natural enzymes in the fields of biosensing [12], disease diagnosis [13,14], environmental protection [15,16] and antibacterial [17,18]. Among these nanozymes, carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterials, are gradually developed due to the advantages of simple preparation, easy surface functionalization, low cost, low toxicity, high stability and good biocompatibility [19–21]. And the similarities between CDs and natural enzymes in terms of size, shape and surface charge give CDs a possibility of intrinsic enzyme-like activities [22]. Besides, the enzyme-like activity of CDs can be further improved by doping heteroatoms, especially transition metal elements [23]. Bai et al. [24] found that the high enzyme-like activity of transition metal-doped CDs might be related to the reduction of reaction activation energy caused by the unfilled molecular orbits of the transition element accepting the lone pair electrons of oxygen. To date, most studies have shown that the prepared CDs display the peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity [22,23,25]. However, there are few studies on the synthesis of CDs with oxidase-like (OD-like) activity or both OD-like and POD-like activities, which immensely restrict the further application of CDs in catalysis. Hence, it is of great significance to synthesize novel CDs doped by transition metal with excellent OD-like and POD-like activities.

As a new class of green solvents, deep eutectic solvents (DESs), which are synthesized by appropriate eutectic proportions of hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and hydrogen bond donor (HBD), have attracted widespread attention. Owing to the favorable properties of low toxicity, great biocompatibility and inexpensive, DESs are widely used in the fields of extraction [26,27], catalysis [28,29], shape-controlled synthesis of nanoparticles [30,31] and electrochemistry [32,33]. In recent years, some studies have shown that CDs synthesized with DESs as precursors present enzyme-like activities [34,35]. For example, Wang et al. [35] prepared the N, Cl-CDs with POD-like activity using DESs. However, the synthesis of CDs with both OD-like and POD-like activities by DESs is rarely reported.

Hydroquinone (HQ), a phenolic compound, is widely used as chemical raw material and synthetic intermediate in the fields of dyes, plastics, rubber and pesticides [36]. Nevertheless, HQ with high toxicity and low degradability can not only pollute the environment, but also seriously affect human health, causing headache, convulsions, kidney damage, and eventually death [37]. The European Union (EU) and the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have listed HQ as a priority pollutant [36]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely applied in food, medicine, chemical industry and paper due to its excellent antimicrobial and bactericidal properties [38]. The introduction of H2O2 into milk can inhibit or delay the formation of microorganisms [39]. However, excessive H2O2 in milk causes some serious gastrointestinal problems and neurodegenerative disorders on humans [40]. In addition, a large amount of H2O2 emitted in industrial production can also cause enormous damage to organisms in the environment. Therefore, it is of great necessity to develop facile, sensitive and accurate methods for the determination of HQ and H2O2.

Till now, various analytical methods including capillary electrophoresis [41], chemiluminescence [42,43], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [44,45] and electrochemistry [46,47] have been established for detecting HQ and H2O2. Although these methods exhibit excellent sensitivity and specificity for the analysis of HQ and H2O2, there are also some limitations, such as expensive instruments, complicated operations, time-consuming and complex sample preparation procedures. By comparison, the colorimetric assay based on nanozymes has developed into a powerful tool for the detection of analytes because of its simplicity, rapidness, low cost, and high sensitivity.

In this study, we primarily synthesized a kind of metallic DESs (MDESs) using CuCl2.2 H2O and ethylene glycol as HBDs and choline chloride as HBA. Then, Cu and Cl co-doped CDs (Cu, Cl-CDs) were synthesized for the first time by one-pot hydrothermal method with MDESs as green solvent and passivation agent and citric acid as carbon source. And then, the enzyme-like activity of the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs was assessed by using 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a substrate. Stupendously, Cu, Cl-CDs could oxidize colorless TMB and generate blue oxidized product (oxTMB) at 652 nm in the absence and presence of H2O2, demonstrating that the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs possessed the OD-like and POD-like catalytic activities ( Scheme 1). Furthermore, two colorimetric sensing platforms were constructed for the first time based on the great OD-like and POD-like catalytic activities of Cu, Cl-CDs, and were applied to determine HQ and H2O2 in real samples, respectively. More importantly, this analytical strategy not only exhibits the advantages of good selectivity, low cost, simple operation, reliable results and high stability, but also meets the requirements of “green chemistry”, which indicates that the CDs prepared based on MDESs have broad application prospects in catalysis and analysis fields.

Citric acid (98%) was provided by Bide Pharmatech Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), anhydrous sodium acetate (NaAc) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, HPLC grade) were obtained from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2.2 H2O, analytical grade) was acquired from Ruijinte Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hydroquinone (HQ, 99%) was purchased from Rhawn Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid (HAc) was provided by Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Choline chloride (98%), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 98%), o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, 98%) and 2,2′- Azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (ABTS, 98%) were bought from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents used in this experiment were of analytical grade and did not require further purification.

The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and the fluorescence spectra were performed with UV-5500PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments Co., Ltd. China) and F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd. China), respectively. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was recorded by a Tecnai G2F30 instrument (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was characterized by Nicolet IN10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum was gained by A300 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

MDESs-assisted synthesis of Cu, Cl-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. MDESs were synthesized by the hydrogen bonds forming with ethylene glycol and CuCl2.2 H2O as HBDs and choline chloride as HBA at a molar ratio of 4:1:1. Briefly, 2.48 g of ethylene glycol, 1.70 g of CuCl2.2 H2O and 1.40 g of choline chloride were stirred continuously at 50 °C for 30 min until a clear and transparent solution appeared. Then, 1 g of citric acid and 10 mL of water were added to the MDESs solution. At the same temperature, the mixed solution was stirred well until it was homogeneous and clear. And then, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was cooled to room temperature, the resulting solution was collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtration with a 0.22 µm filter membrane. To eliminate other impurities, the Cu, Cl-CDs solution was purified through a dialysis membrane (500 Da) for 12 h. Finally, the powder of Cu, Cl-CDs was obtained after freeze-drying and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of dual-enzyme-like activities of Cu, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu, Cl-CDs with OD-like activity was evaluated by using TMB, OPD and ABTS as chromogenic substrates and recording the absorbance values at 652, 450 and 420 nm with UV–vis spectrophotometer. In brief, 150 µL of Cu, Cl-CDs (1.25 mg/mL) was added to 750 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4, 0.2 M), and then mixed with 100 µL of 10 mM TMB, 10 mM OPD and 15 mM ABTS, respectively. Finally, these mixtures were incubated at 50 °C for 30 min, and the corresponding UV–vis spectra were recorded.

The Cu, Cl-CDs with POD-like activity was verified via the oxidation of TMB, OPD and ABTS in the presence of H2O2. In this part of the experiment, 100 µL of Cu, Cl-CDs (1 mg/mL), 250 µL of H2O2 (600 µM) and 100 µL of 10 mM TMB, 10 mM OPD or 15 mM ABTS were added to 550 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4, 0.2 M), and then the mixed solutions were conducted at 45 °C for 20 min. And the absorbance values at 652, 450 and 420 nm were measured, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic analysis of Cu, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The steady-state kinetic analysis of the OD-like performance of Cu, Cl-CDs was carried out at 50 ℃ for 10 min using 150 µL of Cu, Cl-CDs (1.5 mg/mL) with 100 µL of various concentrations of TMB in 750 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4, 0.2 M). Similarly, the steady-state kinetic assays of the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs were performed by changing the concentration of TMB and fixing the concentration of H2O2, or vice versa. And this reaction was conducted at 45 ℃ for 10 min in 550 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4, 0.2 M) with 100 µL of Cu, Cl-CDs (1 mg/mL). Finally, the Michaelis-Menten parameters including K
m and V
max were calculated according to the Lineweaver-Burk plot, as follows:
1

K

Where V and V
max represent the initial and maximal reaction velocity, [S] corresponds to the substrate concentration and K
m is the Michaelis constant.

A typical colorimetric analysis of HQ was established based on the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs. Briefly, 150 µL of Cu, Cl-CDs (1.25 mg/mL), 100 µL of TMB (10 mM) and 400 µL of HQ with various concentrations were added to 350 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4, 0.2 M). Then, the mixtures were reacted at 50 ℃ for 30 min, and the absorbance values of the reaction solutions at 652 nm were measured.

The limit of detection (LOD) of this colorimetric method for HQ was calculated according to the following formula:
LOD=
3 σ
/
k
Where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank samples, and k is the slope of the linear equation.
Simultaneously, to verify the selectivity of this colorimetric method for determining HQ, the absorbance at 652 nm was measured by replacing HQ with some potential interfering substances.

And the applicability of the proposed method for the determination of HQ in tap water, river water and lake water samples (Benxi, China) was investigated. All the water samples were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with 0.22 µm filter membrane. After adjusting the pH of the pretreated samples to the optimum, the concentrations of HQ were determined by the method described above.

Colorimetric detection of H2O2 was carried out based on the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs. In a typical procedure, 100 µL of Cu, Cl-CDs (1 mg/mL) and 100 µL of TMB (10 mM) were added to 550 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4, 0.2 M). Subsequently, 250 µL of H2O2 with various concentrations was introduced, followed by reaction at 45 ℃ for 20 min. And the corresponding absorbance values of the mixed solutions were recorded.
The LOD of this strategy for H2O2 was also calculated according to the 3σ/k rule.
Similarly, the H2O2 was replaced with various potential interferences such as ions, amino acids and reducing substances to evaluate the selectivity of the established method for H2O2.

And the practical applicability of this strategy was evaluated by detecting H2O2 in real water and milk samples (Benxi, China). After centrifugation and filtration, the pH of the tap water and river water samples was adjusted to optimum with HAc-NaAc buffer solution. And the milk sample bought from local supermarket was pretreated with trichloroacetic acid to precipitate proteins based on the previously reported method [48]. Initially, 1 mL of 10-fold diluted milk sample and 2 mL of 5% trichloroacetic acid were thoroughly mixed and sonicated for 20 min. After that, the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtration with 0.22 µm filter membrane. Finally, the concentrations of H2O2 in the pretreated water and milk samples were detected according to the method mentioned above.

The characteristics of the synthesized Cu, Cl-CDs were analyzed by TEM, FT-IR and XPS. Firstly, the morphology of Cu, Cl-CDs was analyzed by the TEM. Fig. 1 A revealed that the Cu, Cl-CDs possessed good dispersity, and the size distribution was in the range of 1.76–5.33 nm with a mean diameter of 3.09 nm (Fig. 1B). And it could be clearly seen from the inset of Fig. 1 A that there was an obvious lattice spacing of 0.22 nm in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of Cu, Cl-CDs, matching the (100) facet of graphite [49].

To confirm the surface structure of Cu, Cl-CDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed. As displayed in Fig. 1 C, the characteristic absorption peaks at 3392, 2926 and 1732 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H, C-H and CO, respectively [22]. The typical absorption bands of C-N and C-O bonds stretching vibrations were located at 1474 and 1079 cm−1 [50]. And the peaks at 1383 and 571 cm−1 were assigned to Cu-O/Cu-N and C-Cl bonds [51], indicating that Cu and Cl combined with CDs successfully.

The XPS spectra were used to further analyze the chemical compositions of Cu, Cl-CDs. The full XPS spectrum of Cu, Cl-CDs ( Fig. 2 A) depicted five typical peaks located at 285, 401, 531, 198 and 932 eV, which indicated the presence of C (60.29%), N (1.99%), O (33.55%), Cl (3.25%) and Cu (0.91%) elements in the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2B) consisting of five peaks at 283.8, 284.6, 285.6, 287.3 and 287.9 eV were related to the C-C/CC, C-N, C-O, C-Cl and CO/CN, respectively [50]. Fig. 2 C revealed the N 1 s spectrum could be subdivided into three peaks with binding energies at 398.6, 400.8 and 401.3 eV, demonstrating the presence of pyridinic N, graphitic N and N-H [50]. The spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 2D) exhibited three peaks at 530.5, 531.5 and 532.5 eV, which were ascribed to Cu-O, CO and C-OH groups, respectively [52,53]. The two peaks at 196.8 and 198.7 eV in the Cl 2p spectrum (Fig. 2E) were derived from C-Cl and N-Cl [53]. In the spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. 2 F), the five characteristic peaks of 931.8, 934.1, 940.4, 951.5 and 953.6 eV were associated with the Cu 2p3/2 features of Cu+ and Cu2+, Cu2+ satellite, Cu 2p1/2 features of Cu0 and Cu+, respectively [22]. In short, these results were in agreement with the FT-IR data, confirming that Cu and Cl were successfully doped into CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The OD-like activity and catalytic mechanism of Cu, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the OD-like activity of the synthesized Cu, Cl-CDs, three typical chromogenic substrates, including TMB, ABTS and OPD, were employed in the absence of H2O2. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 3 A that the corresponding characteristic absorption peaks appeared at 652, 420 and 450 nm upon introduction of the Cu, Cl-CDs into the chromogenic substrates, accompanied by the colors changed from colorless to blue, green and yellow, respectively. While the three substrates hardly exhibited obvious color changes in the absence of Cu, Cl-CDs. Thus the above results indicated that Cu, Cl-CDs possessed the OD-like activity. In this study, the subsequent experiments were carried out with TMB as the chromogenic substrate. In addition, Fig. 3B revealed that the Cl-CDs, whose synthesis was performed in the absence of CuCl2.2 H2O, cannot catalyze the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB with the characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm, suggesting that the introduction of Cu element gave CDs intrinsic OD-like activity. Meanwhile, a set of control experiments were conducted to verify the role of oxidant (O2) in the oxidation of TMB. As shown in Fig. 3 C, oxTMB in the O2-saturated system displayed the highest absorbance value and the darkest color compared with the air-saturated and N2-saturated systems. Therefore, these results showed that O2 as electron acceptor participated in the oxidation of TMB [54,55], and played a crucial part in the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs.

Studies have shown that nano-catalysts with OD-like activity can activate O2 to form different active free radicals, such as·OH,·O2 - and 1O2 [56]. So we used EPR technology to capture the active free radicals generated in the reaction system to further clarify the catalytic mechanism of the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs. Firstly, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), which could combine with oxygen-centered free radicals to produce free radicals adducts, was selected as spin trap. Then, the deionized water and methanol were used as solvents to capture·OH and·O2 - in the reaction system, respectively. It could be seen from Fig. S1A that there was an obvious typical spectrum of DMPO/·OH adduct with a relative intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 in the Cu, Cl-CDs/DMPO/H2O system, indicating the existence of·OH in the catalytic process [57]. However, Fig. S1B displayed that only a few weak peaks instead of the characteristic peak of DMPO/·O2 - adduct with the intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 [58], appeared in the spectrum of Cu, Cl-CDs/DMPO/methanol system, which demonstrated that·O2 - played a negligible role in this catalytic reaction. Furthermore, we also used 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP) as a capture reagent to investigate whether Cu, Cl-CDs could activate O2 to produce 1O2 in the catalytic system. As presented in Fig. S1C, the typical characteristic peak of 2,2,6,6- tetramethyl-4-piperidone-N-oxyl (TEMPO) adduct with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 was observed, which suggested the presence of 1O2 in this system [59]. In brief, the above results indicated that the catalytic mechanism of OD-like activity was mainly derived from·OH and 1O2, which were generated by the reduction of O2 adsorbed on the surface of Cu, Cl-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The POD-like activity and catalytic mechanism of Cu, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The POD-like activity of the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs was assessed using TMB as substrate in the presence of H2O2. It could be seen from Fig. 3D that the absorbance value of oxTMB in the Cu, Cl-CDs/TMB/H2O2 system was higher than that of Cu, Cl-CDs/TMB system originating from OD-like activity, demonstrating the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs. Meanwhile, Fig. 3B showed that the introduction of H2O2 into the Cl-CDs barely catalyzed TMB. In contrast, Cu, Cl-CDs could catalyze the formation of the deep blue oxTMB from TMB in the presence of H2O2, which also suggested that Cu element imparted CDs high POD-like activity. Besides, two other chromogenic substrates, such as ABTS and OPD, were also studied. As depicted in Fig. 3E and F, the corresponding oxidation products in the presence of H2O2 exhibited higher absorption values with deeper color compared with the absence of H2O2, further showing the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs.

Moreover, the POD-like catalytic mechanism of Cu, Cl-CDs was investigated in detail by analyzing the active free radicals produced in the Cu, Cl-CDs/H2O2 system with DMPO and TEMP as radical trappers. As shown in Fig. S1D, E, and F, the characteristic peaks of DMPO/·OH, DMPO/·O2 - and TEMPO adducts were obtained, respectively. It was worth noting that the signal intensities of these adducts were higher than that of adducts formed by the combination of Cu, Cl-CDs and radical trappers in the absence of H2O2. But the peak intensity of DMPO/·O2 - adduct was relatively weak. Therefore, these results confirmed that the mechanism of the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs was derived from the decomposition of H2O2 to generate·OH, 1O2 and a small quantity of·O2 -.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of Cu, Cl-CDs as OD- and POD-mimetics
ce:section.ce:para:
The steady-state kinetics was measured to further fully comprehend the catalytic process of Cu, Cl-CDs. The steady-state kinetic parameters of OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs, including K
m and V
max , were calculated by varying the concentration of TMB according to the Lineweaver-Burk equation. The typical Michaelis-Menten curve and Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal graph of Cu, Cl-CDs were displayed in Fig. 4A and B. And the K
m and V
max values were 0.219 mM and 2.353 × 10−8 Ms−1, respectively. It is well known that the affinity between enzyme and substrate can be reflected by the K
m value. In general, the lower K
m value means the higher affinity between enzyme and substrate. It could be seen from Table S1 that the K
m value of Cu, Cl-CDs was lower than that of other nanozymes with OD-like activity, indicating that Cu, Cl-CDs possessed an excellent affinity for TMB and high catalytic capacity as nanozyme with OD-like activity.

Similarly, the steady-state kinetics of Cu, Cl-CDs with POD-like activity was also determined using TMB and H2O2 as substrates by changing concentration of one substrate at a fixed concentration of the other. Fig. 4C-F depicted the Michaelis-Menten curves and Lineweaver-Burk diagrams of TMB and H2O2, respectively. According to the fitted double reciprocal graphs, the K
m values employing TMB and H2O2 as substrates were computed to be 0.271 and 0.398 mM, respectively, and were lower than the horseradish peroxidase (HRP) in Table S2, which illustrated that Cu, Cl-CDs displayed a higher affinity towards these two substrates than HRP. And the V
max values for TMB and H2O2 were 8.244 × 10−8 and 9.174 × 10−8 Ms−1. Furthermore, compared with other nanozymes (Table S2), the Cu, Cl-CDs exhibited lower K
m value and stronger affinity for the substrates, suggesting the excellent POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HQ based on the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the optimum OD-like catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs, some crucial parameters, such as the concentration of Cu, Cl-CDs, pH, temperature and time were investigated in detail. As presented in Fig. S2A, the effect of the concentration of Cu, Cl-CDs on OD-like catalytic activity was investigated. The results showed that with the concentration of Cu, Cl-CDs increasing to 1.25 mg/mL, the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm reached maximum. Then, the two key factors affecting the catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs, including pH and temperature, were also explored. As displayed in Fig. S2B and C, the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs reached optimum at pH 4.0 and 50 °C. And the relationship between the reaction time and catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs was further studied (Fig. S2D). It could be clearly seen that the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm hardly increased after 30 min. Thus, 1.25 mg/mL of Cu, Cl-CDs, pH 4.0, 50 ℃ and 30 min were chosen as the reaction conditions for the following experiments.

HQ, as a reductant, can significantly inhibit the oxidation of TMB, and then, is oxidized to the p-benzoquinone tautomer by nanozymes. In view of the OD-like catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs, a colorimetric method for sensitive and rapid determination of HQ was established. Fig. 5 A depicted the absorption spectra of oxTMB in Cu, Cl-CDs/TMB system with HQ concentrations from 0 to 120 µM. It could be found that as the concentration of HQ increased, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually decreased with the color changing from blue to colorless. From Fig. 5B, there was a good linearity between the HQ concentrations in the range of 0.1–120 µM and ΔA (ΔA = A (blank) – A (HQ)), with the linear equation of y = 0.0035x + 0.0290 (R2 = 0.9974) and the LOD of 0.08 µM (3σ/k rule). It could be seen from the linear ranges and LODs of various nanomaterials for HQ determination ( Table 1) that the detection performance of Cu, Cl-CDs towards HQ was superior to most previously reported methods.

The effects of some potential interfering substances were carefully studied to investigate the selectivity of Cu, Cl-CDs nanozyme for HQ determination. The absorbance values of these interfering substances including different ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, Ag+, Ba2+, Cu2+, K+, Na+ and Zn2+), amino acids (histidine (His), phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Trp), glycine (Gly), methionine (Met), serine (Ser) and threonine (Thr)) and other substances (catechol (CC), phenol, ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), uric acid (UA) and glucose (Glc)), were presented in Fig. 6 A and B, respectively. It was found that after the introduction of 120 µM HQ, the absorbance at 652 nm decreased significantly due to its strong reducibility. However, the absorbance varied slowly in the presence of these interfering substances (600 µM). Therefore, these results demonstrated that the OD-like catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs was beneficial for sensitive and selective detection of HQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of HQ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed colorimetric strategy based on the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs for the detection of HQ was verified by the standard addition method. After adding various concentrations of HQ (5, 60 and 100 µM) into lake water, river water and tap water samples, the corresponding absorbance values were measured respectively. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries calculated ranged from 96.92% to 102.35% with RSD below 3.79%. These data demonstrated that the constructed Cu, Cl-CDs colorimetric platform in this study was reliable and feasible in HQ determination of practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 based on the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of various experimental conditions on the POD-like catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs was carefully performed. Firstly, the influences of the concentrations of Cu, Cl-CDs and TMB on the catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs were assessed. As illustrated in Fig. S3A and B, the absorbance values of oxTMB reached equilibrium when the concentrations of Cu, Cl-CDs and TMB were 1 mg/mL and 10 mM, respectively. Hence, 1 mg/mL of Cu, Cl-CDs and 10 mM of TMB were adopted as the optimal concentrations. As exhibited in Fig. S3C and D, the catalytic activities of Cu, Cl-CDs increased gradually and then decreased rapidly with the increase of pH and temperature. Therefore, pH 4.0 and 45 °C were used as the optimum conditions for further study. Then, the time variation of oxTMB absorbance from 0 to 80 min was also investigated (Fig. S3E). It could be found that the absorbance at 652 nm increased negligibly after 20 min. So, 20 min was determined as the optimum.

A colorimetric assay for the determination of H2O2 based on the POD-like catalytic activity of Cu, Cl-CDs was constructed. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the introduction of a series of H2O2 concentrations into the Cu, Cl-CDs/TMB system resulted in the constant increase of oxTMB absorbance at 652 nm. It could be seen from Fig. 7B that ΔA (ΔA = A (H2O2) – A (blank)) varied linearly with the concentrations of H2O2 in the range of 1–600 µM. The regression equation was expressed as y = 0.0014x + 0.0591 (R2 = 0.9982) with the LOD of 0.35 µM (3σ/k rule). Comparing this method with most nanozymes for H2O2 detection ( Table 3), it was found that the Cu, Cl-CDs with POD-like catalytic activity exhibited higher sensitivity.

The selectivity of Cu, Cl-CDs based on the POD-like activity for H2O2 detection was assessed by analyzing the influences of potential interfering substances such as amino acids (alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), glutamic acid (Glu), Gly, His, isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), Met, Phe, proline (Pro), Ser, Thr, Trp, tyrosine (Tyr) and valine (Val)), various ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Cl-, NO3 - and SO4 2-) and other substances (Glc, AA, Cys, GSH and Hcy). As illustrated in Fig. 8 A and B, the absorbance value of 600 µM H2O2 was much higher than that of potential interfering substances with the concentration of 1.2 mM. In other words, these interfering substances had negligible effect on the determination of H2O2, suggesting that the Cu, Cl-CDs/TMB system displayed excellent selectivity for H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 in real water and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the concentrations of H2O2 in real water and milk samples were tested to assess the applicability of this established Cu, Cl-CDs/TMB system for H2O2 determination. It could be found from Table 4 that the calculated H2O2 concentrations were comparable to the added concentrations. And the recoveries in the water samples were in the range of 96.87–102.43% with RSD below 3.17%, in milk samples were 98.73–102.45% with RSD below 3.49%. These results indicated that this method based on the POD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs was suitable for H2O2 detection in real samples.

The storage stability of the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs nanozyme was evaluated by placing this nanozyme at room temperature for 60 days. As displayed in Fig. S4A and B, the OD-like and POD-like catalytic activities of Cu, Cl-CDs nanozyme exhibited negligible decrease, which demonstrated that the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs nanozyme possessed excellent stability that ensured the accurate determination of HQ and H2O2.

In conclusion, Cu, Cl-CDs prepared with MDESs as green solvent and doping agent were successfully synthesized for the first time by a hydrothermal method. And we have demonstrated that the prepared Cu, Cl-CDs possessed intrinsic dual-enzyme (OD-like and POD-like) activities and investigated the catalytic mechanism in detail by EPR technology. The results displayed that the OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs came from the·OH and 1O2 radicals, and the POD-like activity was derived from the decomposition of H2O2 to generate the·OH, 1O2 and a small quantity of·O2 -. A simple colorimetric assay for HQ detection was developed on basis of the inhibitive effect of HQ on the excellent OD-like activity of Cu, Cl-CDs, and could be used for the accurate determination of HQ in environmental water. In addition, a colorimetric assay for H2O2 was also constructed based on the POD-like catalytic activity, which was suitable for detecting H2O2 in milk and water. Therefore, this study provides some ideas for the green synthesis of new carbon-based nanozymes, as well as shows potential application prospects in catalytic analysis and environmental monitoring.

Ning Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jianqiang Song: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
